whyWrongByChoice.B: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a non-lactose fermenter and would not produce the metallic green sheen on EMB. It produces blue-green pigment on routine culture media.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Proteus mirabilis is a non-lactose fermenter that does not produce metallic green colonies on EMB agar.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Salmonella typhi is a non-lactose fermenter that would not appear as metallic green on EMB.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: An ICU patient develops MRSA pneumonia. Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic?

explanation.summary: For MRSA pneumonia in hospitalized/ICU patients, vancomycin or linezolid are the agents of choice. MRSA is resistant to all Î²-lactams including nafcillin, cefazolin, and cephalosporins.
explanation.whyCorrect: For suspected or confirmed MRSA pneumonia, vancomycin is the standard empiric choice. Linezolid (a 50S ribosomal subunit inhibitor) is an alternative. MRSA is resistant to methicillin and all Î²-lactam antibiotics, so anti-staphylococcal penicillins (nafcillin) and cephalosporins (cefazolin, ceftriaxone) are ineffective.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nafcillin is an anti-staphylococcal Î²-lactam used for MSSA (methicillin-sensitive S. aureus). MRSA is by definition resistant to methicillin and related Î²-lactams including nafcillin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin used for MSSA infections. MRSA is resistant to all cephalosporins.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is a Î²-lactam/Î²-lactamase inhibitor combination. MRSA resistance is due to altered PBPs (PBP2a), not Î²-lactamase, so clavulanate cannot overcome MRSA resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin. Like all Î²-lactams, it is ineffective against MRSA.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: Nocardia spp. are commonly found in soil and can cause skin infections through direct inoculation. Which of the following patterns of cutaneous spread is characteristic of Nocardia skin infection?

explanation.summary: Nocardia skin infections classically present as a nodular lesion at the inoculation site that spreads along draining lymphatic channels in a sporotrichoid pattern, named for its resemblance to sporotrichosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A classic presentation of cutaneous nocardiosis is a nodular lesion at the inoculation site (often from contaminated soil via thorn prick or wound exposure) that spreads along draining lymphatic channels in a sporotrichoid pattern. This pattern is named for its resemblance to sporotrichosis (caused by the fungus Sporothrix schenckii) and provides an important diagnostic clue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Centrifugal (outward-spreading) rash from the trunk is typical of viral exanthems, not Nocardia skin infections.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dermatomal vesicular eruption is characteristic of herpes zoster (shingles), not bacterial skin infections.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Butterfly distribution on the face is associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, not Nocardia infections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Purpuric lesions on extremities suggest vasculitis, meningococcemia, or DIC, not Nocardia skin infection.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: A hospitalized patient with CAP grows an ESBL-producing E. coli from sputum culture. Which of the following antibiotics is most appropriate for this resistant organism?

explanation.summary: ESBL-producing E. coli are resistant to most Î²-lactams including cephalosporins. Carbapenems like meropenem are preferred due to their stability against ESBL enzymes, broad-spectrum activity, and excellent tissue penetration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Extended-spectrum Î²-lactamase (ESBL)-producing strains of E. coli hydrolyze most Î²-lactam antibiotics including cephalosporins. Carbapenems such as meropenem or imipenem are preferred because they are stable against ESBL enzymes and have broad-spectrum activity with excellent tissue penetration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin that is hydrolyzed by ESBL enzymes. It would be ineffective against ESBL-producing E. coli.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ampicillin is a basic penicillin that is readily hydrolyzed by standard Î²-lactamases and ESBLs. It is ineffective here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azithromycin is a macrolide with no reliable activity against enteric gram-negative bacteria like E. coli.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While amoxicillin-clavulanate contains a Î²-lactamase inhibitor, ESBLs are not reliably inhibited by clavulanate in clinical settings, making this combination unreliable for serious ESBL infections.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: Nocardia spp. possess virulence factors that help them resist phagocytic killing. Which of the following enzymes produced by Nocardia specifically helps neutralize hydrogen peroxide produced by macrophages?

explanation.summary: Nocardia spp. produce catalase, which breaks down Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚ (hydrogen peroxide) produced by macrophages as part of the oxidative burst. This provides further resistance to phagocytic killing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nocardia spp. produce catalase, which breaks down hydrogen peroxide (Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚), a key component of the macrophage oxidative burst. Combined with mycolic acid in the cell wall (which resists destruction inside macrophages), catalase production provides additional resistance to phagocytic killing. Nocardia can survive both inside and outside host cells (facultative intracellular).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coagulase is produced by S. aureus to clot plasma and form a protective fibrin shield. It is not a Nocardia virulence factor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyaluronidase degrades hyaluronic acid in connective tissue, aiding bacterial spread. While some bacteria produce it, it is not the specific enzyme that neutralizes Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚ in Nocardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Collagenase degrades collagen in connective tissue. It is not the enzyme that specifically neutralizes macrophage Hâ‚‚Oâ‚‚.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Streptokinase is produced by streptococci to dissolve fibrin clots. It is not a Nocardia virulence factor.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: An IV drug user presents with high fever, a new heart murmur, and multiple bilateral lung nodules on chest CT. Blood cultures grow gram-positive cocci in clusters. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right-sided endocarditis with septic emboli from S. aureus

explanation.summary: IV drug users are at high risk for right-sided infective endocarditis caused by S. aureus. Septic emboli from tricuspid valve vegetations shower to the lungs, causing multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules.
explanation.whyCorrect: S. aureus poses extra risk in IV drug users, who are susceptible to soft tissue infections and infective endocarditis. Right-sided endocarditis (tricuspid valve) causes septic emboli to the lungs, appearing as multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules. The gram-positive cocci in clusters confirm S. aureus, and the new heart murmur plus IV drug use history clinch the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: S. pneumoniae causes lobar pneumonia, not multiple bilateral nodules with a new heart murmur. It is also lancet-shaped diplococci, not cocci in clusters.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Miliary TB shows tiny diffuse granulomatous lesions. It is caused by acid-fast bacilli, not gram-positive cocci in clusters.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary aspergillosis affects immunocompromised patients and would show fungal elements, not gram-positive cocci in blood cultures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Viral pneumonia typically shows bilateral interstitial or ground-glass opacities, not discrete nodules. Blood cultures would not grow gram-positive cocci in viral pneumonia.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: Both E. coli and K. pneumoniae are lactose-fermenting gram-negative rods. Which of the following tests best differentiates E. coli from K. pneumoniae?

explanation.summary: E. coli is indole-positive (converts tryptophan to indole, detected by Kovac's reagent turning red), while K. pneumoniae is indole-negative. This is a key differentiating test between these two lactose-fermenting gram-negative rods.
explanation.whyCorrect: E. coli is typically motile and converts tryptophan to indole (indole-positive). Kovac's reagent reacts with indole and turns red, providing a positive result. K. pneumoniae is indole-negative. Since both organisms are lactose-fermenting gram-negative rods, the indole test is a key differentiating test.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both E. coli and K. pneumoniae are catalase-positive, so this test does not differentiate between them.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Both E. coli and K. pneumoniae are oxidase-negative gram-negative enteric bacteria. The oxidase test does not distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The coagulase test distinguishes S. aureus (coagulase-positive) from other staphylococci. It is not used for gram-negative rod differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The CAMP test is used to identify S. agalactiae (Group B Strep). It is not relevant for differentiating gram-negative enteric bacteria.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: A patient with aspiration pneumonia has foul-smelling sputum and a right lower lobe infiltrate. Which of the following groups of bacteria is most likely responsible for the malodorous sputum?

explanation.summary: Aspiration pneumonia from oral anaerobes (Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) characteristically produces extremely foul-smelling sputumâ€”the same bacteria that cause bad breath (halitosis).
explanation.whyCorrect: The extremely foul-smelling sputum is characteristic of aspiration pneumonia caused by oral anaerobes. These include Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Fusobacterium, and Bacteroides species. The sputum has a pungent odor because these are the same bacteria that cause bad breath (halitosis). Most aspiration pneumonia infections are mixed (multiple species).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atypical bacteria cause dry, nonproductive cough with mild symptoms. They do not produce foul-smelling sputum.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pseudomonas causes a fruity-smelling (like wine or Fritos) odor in culture, not the characteristically foul-smelling sputum of anaerobic aspiration pneumonia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. pneumoniae can cause aspiration pneumonia but does not produce the characteristically foul-smelling sputum associated with oral anaerobes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nocardia causes a subacute pneumonia mimicking TB in immunocompromised patients. It is not associated with foul-smelling sputum from aspiration.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: A patient with MSSA pneumonia (confirmed methicillin-sensitive) is being treated intravenously. Which of the following is the most appropriate targeted antibiotic?

explanation.summary: For confirmed MSSA infections, anti-staphylococcal Î²-lactams such as nafcillin or cefazolin are the preferred IV treatment. These are more effective against MSSA than vancomycin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methicillin-sensitive S. aureus (MSSA) is best treated with anti-staphylococcal Î²-lactams such as nafcillin or cefazolin (IV formulations). These agents have superior efficacy against MSSA compared to vancomycin, which should be reserved for MRSA or patients with Î²-lactam allergy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vancomycin is effective against MSSA but is not the preferred agent. Anti-staphylococcal Î²-lactams like nafcillin have superior outcomes for MSSA infections. Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Linezolid is used for MRSA infections. For MSSA, anti-staphylococcal Î²-lactams are preferred for their superior efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Daptomycin is used for serious MRSA infections but is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant, making it ineffective for pneumonia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TMP-SMX can treat community-acquired MRSA skin infections but is not the optimal choice for MSSA pneumonia requiring IV therapy.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: S. aureus accounts for only 1-5% of community-acquired pneumonia. However, approximately what percentage of S. aureus pneumonia cases occur in the post-influenza setting?

explanation.summary: While S. aureus is an uncommon cause of CAP overall (1-5%), 70-80% of S. aureus pneumonia cases are post-influenza secondary bacterial pneumonia.
explanation.whyCorrect: S. aureus only accounts for approximately 1-5% of CAP, but 70-80% of S. aureus pneumonia is post-influenza. This makes it a critically important pathogen to consider in patients who develop bacterial pneumonia following influenza. MRSA is a major concern, especially in HAP/VAP and IV drug users.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 10-20% significantly underestimates the proportion of S. aureus pneumonia that is post-influenza.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 30-40% underestimates the strong association between S. aureus pneumonia and preceding influenza infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50-60% is still below the actual proportion of 70-80%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90-100% overestimates. While the majority of S. aureus pneumonia is post-influenza, some cases occur in other settings (IV drug users, nosocomial).

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: Nocardia spp. infections primarily occur in patients with deficiencies in which arm of the immune system?

explanation.summary: Nocardia spp. are facultative intracellular organisms, so loss of T cell immunity (cell-mediated immunity) is strongly associated with nocardiosis. Risk factors include chronic steroid use, organ transplantation, and HIV/AIDS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nocardia spp. can live both inside and outside host cells (facultative intracellular). Because intracellular survival is a key part of their pathogenesis, loss of T cell immunity (cell-mediated immunity) is strongly associated with nocardiosis. Risk factors reflecting T cell impairment include chronic steroid use, organ transplantation, and HIV/AIDS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Humoral immunity (B cell) deficiency is more associated with encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae). Nocardia pathogenesis depends on intracellular survival, requiring T cell control.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complement deficiency increases susceptibility to encapsulated organisms and Neisseria, not typically Nocardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neutrophil dysfunction (as in CGD) is more associated with catalase-positive organisms like S. aureus and Aspergillus. While neutrophils contribute to Nocardia defense, T cell immunity is the primary determinant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mucosal IgA deficiency increases susceptibility to mucosal infections but is not the primary immune deficit associated with nocardiosis.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: A patient has S. aureus isolated from sputum. The lab performs mannitol salt agar (MSA) testing. Yellow colonies on MSA confirm which of the following?

explanation.summary: S. aureus ferments mannitol on mannitol salt agar, producing yellow colonies. MSA is selective (high salt concentration selects for staphylococci) and differential (mannitol fermentation differentiates S. aureus from coagulase-negative staphylococci).
explanation.whyCorrect: S. aureus ferments mannitol on mannitol salt agar (MSA), turning the colonies and surrounding agar yellow due to acid production. MSA is both selective (7.5% NaCl selects for salt-tolerant staphylococci) and differential (mannitol fermentation distinguishes S. aureus from coagulase-negative staphylococci, which do not ferment mannitol).

whyWrongByChoice.A: MSA does not test for methicillin resistance. Methicillin resistance is determined by oxacillin/cefoxitin disk testing, MIC testing, or detection of mecA gene/PBP2a.
whyWrongByChoice.C: MSA does not detect Î²-lactamase production. Î²-lactamase testing requires specific enzyme assays.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Yellow colonies on MSA indicate mannitol fermentation, which is characteristic of coagulase-POSITIVE S. aureus, not coagulase-negative species.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MSA does not test for vancomycin susceptibility. Vancomycin susceptibility is determined by MIC testing.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: Which of the following features best distinguishes Nocardia spp. from Actinomyces israelii on laboratory testing?

explanation.summary: Both Nocardia and Actinomyces form branching filaments, but Nocardia is aerobic and weakly acid-fast (due to mycolic acid), while Actinomyces is anaerobic and NOT acid-fast. This is the key distinguishing feature.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both Nocardia and Actinomyces are gram-positive bacteria that form branching filaments. The key distinguishing features are: Nocardia is aerobic and weakly acid-fast (due to mycolic acid in the cell wall), while Actinomyces is anaerobic and NOT acid-fast. This distinction is a classic board question.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both Nocardia and Actinomyces appear as gram-positive branching filamentous rods on Gram stain. Morphology alone cannot distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Both organisms form branching filaments. This is a shared feature, not a distinguishing one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While Nocardia is catalase-positive, catalase testing alone is not the primary distinguishing feature taught for these organisms. The oxygen requirement and acid-fast staining are more definitive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both organisms can eventually grow on routine agars, though Nocardia grows aerobically and Actinomyces anaerobically. The growth atmosphere difference is captured in the correct answer.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: K. pneumoniae has a prominent mucoid polysaccharide capsule that gives it a distinctive appearance in culture. Which of the following laboratory features is most characteristic?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Medusa head colonies on blood agar

explanation.summary: K. pneumoniae has a mucoid polysaccharide capsule that gives it a characteristic thick, snotty (mucoid) appearance in culture. This correlates with its ability to produce thick, mucoid sputum ('currant jelly') in vivo.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both E. coli and K. pneumoniae have capsules, but K. pneumoniae has a particularly prominent mucoid capsule. This gives K. pneumoniae a thick, snotty appearance in culture, reflecting the same mucoid quality seen in the 'currant jelly' sputum produced in vivo.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blue-green pigment is characteristic of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (from pyocyanin and pyoverdine), not K. pneumoniae.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Satellite colonies near S. aureus are characteristic of Haemophilus influenzae, which requires factors V and X released by hemolytic bacteria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fried-egg colonies on Eaton agar are characteristic of Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Medusa head colonies on blood agar are characteristic of Bacillus anthracis.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: For severe disseminated nocardiosis with brain abscesses, which of the following combination regimens would be most appropriate?

explanation.summary: For severe Nocardia infections with dissemination (e.g., brain abscesses), TMP-SMX monotherapy is insufficient. Combination therapy with agents such as amikacin, imipenem, or linezolid is required due to variable resistance patterns.
explanation.whyCorrect: While TMP-SMX is first-line for Nocardia, mild-to-moderate pulmonary infections may be treated with monotherapy. For severe infections, especially disseminated disease with brain abscesses, combination therapy is required. TMP-SMX plus agents such as amikacin, imipenem, or linezolid are used due to variable resistance patterns and the organism's tendency to cause chronic, relapsing disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: TMP-SMX alone is appropriate for mild-to-moderate pulmonary nocardiosis but insufficient for severe disseminated disease with brain abscesses.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vancomycin plus cefepime is used for nosocomial infections with MRSA/Pseudomonas concern, not for Nocardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azithromycin plus ethambutol is the MAC treatment regimen, not for Nocardia infections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rifampin plus isoniazid is a latent TB treatment regimen, not for Nocardia.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: The clinical presentation of pulmonary nocardiosis can mimic tuberculosis. Which of the following clinical features is shared between these two conditions?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fever, weight loss, cough, and cavitary lesions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Currant jelly sputum with upper lobe involvement

explanation.summary: Pulmonary nocardiosis presents as subacute or chronic pneumonia with fever, weight loss, productive cough (sometimes bloody), and cavitary or nodular lung lesions on imagingâ€”features that closely parallel tuberculosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most cases of pulmonary nocardiosis present as subacute or chronic pneumonia with presentations that parallel tuberculosis: fever, weight loss, and cough (typically productive, sometimes with hemoptysis). On imaging, cavitary or nodular lung lesions may be present, further mimicking TB. Laboratory identification is necessary to distinguish the two.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both nocardiosis and TB typically have a subacute or chronic course, not acute onset. Acute lobar consolidation is more typical of S. pneumoniae.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bilateral wheezing with interstitial opacities is characteristic of atypical/viral pneumonia, not pulmonary nocardiosis or TB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Currant jelly sputum with upper lobe involvement is characteristic of K. pneumoniae, not Nocardia or TB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A self-limiting 3-6 day illness describes Pontiac fever (Legionella) or viral upper respiratory infections, not the chronic course of nocardiosis or TB.

questions.topicName: Nocardia spp., E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus
questions.stem: E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus can all cause pneumonia, but most cases are hospital-acquired rather than community-acquired. In which of the following settings is Pseudomonas spp. a MORE common cause than these three organisms?

explanation.summary: While E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus can cause VAP, Pseudomonas spp. is a more common cause of ventilator-associated pneumonia and is the most common cause of MDR VAP.
explanation.whyCorrect: K. pneumoniae and E. coli are strongly associated with immunocompromised patients and HAP, especially in patients on ventilators. However, Pseudomonas spp. is a more common cause of VAP. As discussed in the Pseudomonas brick, P. aeruginosa is the most common cause of multidrug-resistant VAP due to its intrinsic resistance, biofilm formation on endotracheal tubes, and hospital ubiquity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Post-influenza secondary pneumonia is most commonly caused by S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and H. influenzaeâ€”not Pseudomonas.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Neonatal pneumonia is most commonly caused by E. coli and Group B Streptococcus, not Pseudomonas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspiration pneumonia in alcoholics is most associated with K. pneumoniae and oral anaerobes, not Pseudomonas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Outpatient CAP is most commonly caused by S. pneumoniae, atypical bacteria, and respiratory virusesâ€”not Pseudomonas, which is primarily nosocomial.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A livestock worker presents with a rapidly enlarging painless papule on his forearm that has progressed to an ulcer with a black necrotic eschar surrounded by edema. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Cutaneous anthrax (~95% of anthrax cases) presents as a rapidly growing painless papule that progresses to an ulcer with a characteristic black necrotic eschar. Livestock workers are at highest risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cutaneous anthrax is the most common form of anthrax (~95% of cases). It presents as a rapidly growing papule with surrounding edema that progresses to a painless ulcer with a black necrotic eschar. It occurs in exposed areas (face, neck, arms, hands) after B. anthracis spores penetrate through skin breaks. Livestock workers in rural settings are at highest risk. Without treatment, mortality is 10-40%; with antibiotics, <1%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ecthyma gangrenosum is caused by Pseudomonas bacteremia, presenting as hemorrhagic/necrotic lesions in immunocompromised patients. It is not associated with livestock exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Brown recluse spider bites can cause necrotic skin lesions, but the occupational livestock exposure and the characteristic painless black eschar point to cutaneous anthrax.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pyoderma gangrenosum is an inflammatory skin condition associated with IBD, not an infectious process from livestock exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome causes diffuse skin exfoliation in neonates/young children, not localized necrotic eschars in adults.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A patient presents with nonspecific symptoms of fever, myalgias, and chest discomfort that rapidly progress to respiratory failure. Chest X-ray shows widening of the mediastinum. Which of the following forms of anthrax is most consistent with this presentation?

explanation.summary: Inhalation anthrax occurs when spores are inhaled and transported to mediastinal lymph nodes. The classic finding is widening of the mediastinum due to hemorrhagic mediastinitis. Untreated mortality exceeds 90%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhalation anthrax occurs when spores are inhaled, taken up by alveolar macrophages, and transported to mediastinal lymph nodes where they germinate and proliferate. This leads to toxin-mediated hemorrhagic mediastinitis, causing the classic finding of mediastinal widening on chest X-ray. Rapid progression to necrotizing pneumonia, bacteremia, and meningitis is common. Untreated mortality exceeds 90%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cutaneous anthrax presents as a localized skin lesion with a black eschar, not mediastinal widening or respiratory failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: GI anthrax presents with abdominal pain, vomiting, bloody diarrhea, and necrosis of the GI tract, not mediastinal widening.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Injectional anthrax occurs in IV drug users and presents with soft tissue infection and edema at the injection site, not mediastinal widening.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Primary meningeal anthrax is extremely rare and presents with meningeal signs and altered consciousness, not primarily with mediastinal widening.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: B. anthracis produces a tripartite anthrax toxin. Which of the following components serves as the common host-cell binding (B) component for both lethal toxin and edema toxin?

explanation.summary: Protective antigen (PA) is the common binding (B) component of both anthrax toxins. It binds host cell receptors and forms an endosomal pore allowing lethal factor and edema factor to access the cytosol.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anthrax toxin consists of three secreted proteins: protective antigen (PA), lethal factor (LF), and edema factor (EF). PA is the common host-cell binding (B) component of both toxins. It binds to cell receptors and forms an endosomal pore, allowing LF and EF (the active A components) to enter the cytosol. Lethal toxin = PA + LF; Edema toxin = PA + EF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lethal factor is the active (A) component of lethal toxin (LT). It is a protease that cleaves MAPK pathway enzymes, causing cell death. It is not the binding component.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Edema factor is the active (A) component of edema toxin (ET). It is an adenylate cyclase that increases cAMP. It is not the binding component.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The poly-D-glutamate capsule is a separate virulence factor encoded by pXO2 that inhibits phagocytosis. It is not part of the anthrax toxin system.
whyWrongByChoice.E: B. anthracis is gram-positive and does not possess LPS. LPS is a component of gram-negative bacteria.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A 57-year-old sheep farmer presents with a 9-day history of fever, headache, myalgia, and non-productive cough. Chest X-ray shows patchy infiltrates. Blood tests reveal slightly elevated liver enzymes, but cultures are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Q fever (C. burnetii) is an occupational disease of livestock workers. It presents with flu-like symptoms, atypical pneumonia, and mild hepatitis (elevated liver enzymes). Blood cultures are typically negative because the organism is obligately intracellular.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sheep farmer occupation, flu-like illness with atypical pneumonia, elevated liver enzymes (mild hepatitis), and negative blood cultures are classic for Q fever (C. burnetii). C. burnetii is an obligate intracellular pathogen whose primary reservoir is livestock. It causes flu-like symptoms, pneumonia, and hepatitis. Blood cultures are negative because the organism cannot grow on routine media.

whyWrongByChoice.A: TB presents with chronic cough, weight loss, and night sweats, not the acute flu-like onset described. TB is also not specifically associated with sheep farming.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhalation anthrax presents with mediastinal widening and rapid progression to respiratory failure, not the subacute course with mild hepatitis seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Legionnaires' disease presents with severe pneumonia, high fever, GI symptoms, and hyponatremia. While liver enzymes can be elevated, the occupation and mild course favor Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Influenza pneumonia presents acutely with high fever and respiratory symptoms but is not typically associated with elevated liver enzymes or an occupational exposure to livestock.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: The capsule of B. anthracis is composed of poly-D-glutamate, which is unusual because most bacterial capsules are composed of polysaccharides. Which of the following is the primary function of this protein capsule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhances binding to host cell receptors

explanation.summary: The poly-D-glutamate capsule of B. anthracis is anti-phagocytic, allowing evasion of macrophages and promoting bloodstream dissemination. It is one of only two medically relevant bacteria with a protein-based capsule (the other is Yersinia pestis).
explanation.whyCorrect: The B. anthracis capsule, composed of poly-D-glutamate (a protein), is anti-phagocytic, allowing the bacteria to evade macrophages and promote bloodstream dissemination. This is encoded by the pXO2 plasmid. Notably, few medically relevant bacteria have protein-based capsules; Yersinia pestis is the only other notable example with its F1 protein capsule.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The capsule does not enhance host cell receptor binding. The toxin system (via protective antigen) handles cell binding. The capsule's role is immune evasion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The capsule is produced in the vegetative state after spore germination, not during spore formation. Spore formation is a distinct process.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Biofilm formation is not the primary function of the B. anthracis capsule. The capsule's role is anti-phagocytic immune evasion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The capsule does not directly mediate antibiotic resistance. It functions to evade the host immune system.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Edema factor (EF), one of the active components of anthrax toxin, functions as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A protease cleaving MAPK pathway enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: An adenylate cyclase increasing cellular cAMP

explanation.summary: Edema factor (EF) is an adenylate cyclase that increases cellular cAMP, causing edema and disruption of innate immunity. It is the active (A) component of edema toxin (PA + EF).
explanation.whyCorrect: Edema factor, once it enters the cell via the PA pore, becomes an adenylate cyclase that increases cellular cAMP levels. This causes edema and disrupts innate immunity. EF is the active (A) component of edema toxin (ET = PA + EF).

whyWrongByChoice.A: A protease cleaving MAPK pathway enzymes describes lethal factor (LF), not edema factor. LF is the active component of lethal toxin (LT = PA + LF).
whyWrongByChoice.C: EF is not a DNase. It is an adenylate cyclase that elevates intracellular cAMP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A phospholipase degrading cell membranes describes the mechanism of phospholipase C (a Pseudomonas virulence factor), not edema factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Superantigens are produced by some S. aureus strains and Group A Streptococcus. EF does not function as a superantigen.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: B. anthracis forms characteristic colonies on blood agar. Which of the following best describes the colony morphology?

explanation.summary: B. anthracis forms characteristic 'Medusa head' colonies on blood agar. It is non-hemolytic (Î³-hemolytic), distinguishing it from B. cereus and other Bacillus species which are Î²-hemolytic and motile.
explanation.whyCorrect: B. anthracis is identified as a large, facultative anaerobic, Gram-positive, non-hemolytic, spore-forming rod that forms characteristic 'Medusa head' colonies on blood agar. The Medusa head appearance is due to the irregular, wavy colony edges. B. anthracis is non-hemolytic (Î³-hemolytic) and nonmotile, distinguishing it from B. cereus (Î²-hemolytic, motile).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mucoid, blue-green pigmented colonies are characteristic of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (from pyocyanin), not B. anthracis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-hemolytic, golden-yellow colonies describe S. aureus, not B. anthracis. B. anthracis is specifically non-hemolytic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Satellite colonies near hemolytic bacteria are characteristic of H. influenzae, which requires factors V and X released by hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fried-egg colonies on special agar describe Mycoplasma pneumoniae on Eaton agar.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: C. burnetii forms a spore-like structure that allows it to persist in the environment. Which of the following best describes this organism's classification and intracellular behavior?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obligate aerobe replicating on cell surfaces

explanation.summary: C. burnetii is an obligate intracellular bacterium that replicates within vacuoles (phagolysosomes) of host cells. It forms a spore-like small-cell variant that can persist in the environment for months.
explanation.whyCorrect: C. burnetii is an obligate intracellular pathogen that replicates within vacuoles of host cells (specifically in acidic phagolysosomal compartments). When outside of cells, it forms a spore-like structure called a small-cell variant, which allows it to persist outside a host for months under adverse conditions. This resilience makes it a potential bioterrorism agent.

whyWrongByChoice.A: C. burnetii is obligate intracellular (not facultative) and replicates in vacuoles (not the cytoplasm). Some other intracellular pathogens like Rickettsia replicate in the cytoplasm, but C. burnetii is distinct.
whyWrongByChoice.C: C. burnetii is obligately intracellular in its replicative form. While its small-cell variant persists extracellularly, active replication occurs within host cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: C. burnetii does not replicate on cell surfaces. It replicates within intracellular vacuoles (phagolysosomes).
whyWrongByChoice.E: C. burnetii is not a facultative anaerobe. It is an obligate intracellular pathogen. Its persistent form is called a small-cell variant, not a true endospore.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: The first-line treatment for acute Q fever is which of the following?

explanation.summary: Doxycycline is first-line for Q fever because it is highly lipophilic and can penetrate cells to reach C. burnetii in its acidic intracellular compartments (phagolysosomes). It is often combined with hydroxychloroquine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Doxycycline is first-line therapy for Q fever because C. burnetii localizes within acidic intracellular compartments (phagolysosomes), requiring agents with strong intracellular activity. Doxycycline is highly lipophilic, penetrates cells effectively, and accumulates within infected host cells. The preferred regimen combines doxycycline with hydroxychloroquine in non-pregnant individuals.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While fluoroquinolones have intracellular penetration, doxycycline (combined with hydroxychloroquine) is the established first-line regimen for Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amoxicillin is a Î²-lactam that does not achieve adequate intracellular concentrations to reach C. burnetii within phagolysosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vancomycin is used for MRSA and serious gram-positive infections. It has no role in Q fever treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Azithromycin has good intracellular penetration but doxycycline is the established first-line agent for Q fever.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: The pXO1 and pXO2 plasmids are both essential for B. anthracis virulence. Which of the following correctly describes what each plasmid encodes?

choices.label: B
choices.text: pXO1: PA, EF, LF; pXO2: poly-D-glutamate capsule

choices.label: C
choices.text: pXO1: spore coat proteins; pXO2: motility genes

explanation.summary: pXO1 encodes the three anthrax toxin components (PA, EF, LF). pXO2 encodes the poly-D-glutamate capsule. Both plasmids are required for full virulence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pXO1 plasmid encodes protective antigen (PA), edema factor (EF), and lethal factor (LF)â€”the three components of anthrax toxin. The pXO2 plasmid encodes the poly-D-glutamate capsule. Both plasmids are essential for a strain of B. anthracis to be virulent. PCR can detect these plasmids to confirm pathogenic strains.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the plasmid assignments. pXO1 encodes the toxin genes, not the capsule. pXO2 encodes the capsule, not the toxin genes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: pXO1 does not encode spore coat proteins, and pXO2 does not encode motility genes. B. anthracis is actually nonmotile.
whyWrongByChoice.D: pXO1 does not encode Î²-lactamase. It encodes the anthrax toxin components (PA, EF, LF).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neither plasmid encodes adhesins or siderophores. They encode toxin components (pXO1) and the capsule (pXO2).

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Monoclonal antibodies raxibacumab and obiltoxaximab are used in anthrax treatment. Which of the following toxin components do they target?

explanation.summary: Raxibacumab and obiltoxaximab are monoclonal antibodies directed against protective antigen (PA), preventing PA from binding to cellular receptors and thus blocking both lethal toxin and edema toxin entry into cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Raxibacumab and obiltoxaximab are monoclonal antibodies directed against protective antigen (PA), the common binding component of both anthrax toxins. By preventing PA from binding to cell receptors, they block entry of both lethal factor and edema factor into host cells, preventing toxin-mediated damage. They are used in combination with antimicrobial therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While blocking LF would prevent lethal toxin activity, these monoclonal antibodies target PA (the binding component) to block both toxins simultaneously.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Similarly, targeting EF alone would only block edema toxin. Targeting PA blocks both toxins at once.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The poly-D-glutamate capsule is a separate virulence factor not targeted by these monoclonal antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: B. anthracis is gram-positive and does not have LPS.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Chronic Q fever, occurring in 1-5% of patients, can cause which of the following serious complications?

explanation.summary: Chronic Q fever (>6 months) can cause culture-negative infective endocarditis, arterial infections, and osteomyelitis. Patients with valvular damage, immunocompromised, and pregnant women are at highest risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rarely (1-5%), Q fever becomes chronic (>6 months). Severe manifestations include culture-negative infective endocarditis (the most feared complication), arterial infections (especially of grafts or aneurysms), and osteomyelitis. Patients with valvular damage, immunocompromised patients, and pregnant women are at highest risk. Vegetative growths can be small or absent, making diagnosis challenging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hemorrhagic mediastinitis is a complication of inhalation anthrax, not chronic Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Black necrotic skin eschars are characteristic of cutaneous anthrax, not Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Currant jelly sputum is associated with Klebsiella pneumoniae, not Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ring-enhancing brain abscesses are associated with Nocardia, Toxoplasma, and disseminated TB, not typically chronic Q fever. CNS involvement in Q fever (meningitis) is extremely rare.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: For suspected systemic anthrax with inhalation or GI involvement, empiric therapy should include antibiotics from how many different classes?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three: two bactericidal agents plus one protein synthesis inhibitor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Single class with monoclonal antibodies only

explanation.summary: Systemic anthrax requires empiric therapy with two bactericidal agents from different classes plus one protein synthesis inhibitor. A common regimen is meropenem plus ciprofloxacin plus doxycycline for 2-3 weeks.
explanation.whyCorrect: For suspected systemic anthrax (inhalation or GI involvement), treatment should be started immediately with two drugs from different classes with bactericidal activity plus one protein synthesis inhibitor. A commonly used regimen is meropenem (bactericidal) + fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin, bactericidal) + doxycycline or minocycline (protein synthesis inhibitors). Duration is 2-3 weeks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Monotherapy is never appropriate for systemic anthrax given its high mortality. Multiple drug classes are needed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Two bactericidal agents alone are insufficient. The standard approach includes a protein synthesis inhibitor (like doxycycline) in addition to bactericidal agents.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Four-drug therapy like RIPE is for tuberculosis, not anthrax. Anthrax uses three agents from different classes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Monoclonal antibodies (raxibacumab, obiltoxaximab) are antitoxin therapies given IN ADDITION to antimicrobial therapy, not as monotherapy.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with Q fever during pregnancy. TMP-SMX with folic acid is prescribed instead of the standard doxycycline-hydroxychloroquine regimen. Which of the following best explains why doxycycline is avoided?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tetracyclines cause fetal tooth and bone abnormalities

choices.label: C
choices.text: Doxycycline is inactive against C. burnetii in pregnancy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hydroxychloroquine is contraindicated with prenatal vitamins

explanation.summary: Tetracyclines (including doxycycline) are avoided in pregnancy because they can cause permanent tooth discoloration and bone growth abnormalities in the developing fetus. TMP-SMX with folic acid is the alternative for Q fever in pregnancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Doxycycline (a tetracycline) is avoided in pregnancy because tetracyclines can cause permanent discoloration of developing teeth (yellow-brown) and inhibit bone growth in the fetus. For Q fever during pregnancy, TMP-SMX with folic acid supplementation is the preferred alternative. Following delivery, the standard doxycycline-hydroxychloroquine combination can be resumed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While doxycycline can cause some hepatic effects, the primary reason for avoidance in pregnancy is fetal tooth and bone effects, not maternal hepatotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Doxycycline is highly active against C. burnetii regardless of pregnancy status. The issue is fetal toxicity, not reduced efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydroxychloroquine is not specifically contraindicated with prenatal vitamins. The primary concern is tetracycline effects on the fetus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neonatal respiratory depression is more associated with opioids and benzodiazepines, not doxycycline.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Bacillus spp. endospore location can help distinguish them from Clostridium spp. Where are the endospores located in Bacillus spp.?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subterminal, giving a tennis racket shape

explanation.summary: Bacillus spp. have endospores located in the middle (central) of the bacterium. Clostridium spp. have terminal endospores giving a drumstick appearance. This morphological difference helps distinguish the two genera.
explanation.whyCorrect: The endospores of Bacillus spp. are located in the middle (centrally) of the bacterium. In contrast, Clostridium spp. have endospores on the ends (terminal), giving the bacteria a characteristic drumstick appearance. This spore position is a useful morphological feature for laboratory differentiation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Terminal endospores with a drumstick appearance are characteristic of Clostridium spp., not Bacillus spp.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subterminal spores giving a tennis racket shape can be seen in some Clostridium species. Bacillus endospores are centrally located.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bacillus is a sporulating (spore-forming) genus. Endospore formation is a defining characteristic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endospores form inside the bacterial cell, not externally. They are released when the vegetative cell lyses.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: C. burnetii stains poorly with standard Gram stain. Which of the following diagnostic methods is most appropriate for confirming Q fever in a clinical setting?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Giemsa stain of peripheral blood smear

explanation.summary: C. burnetii is diagnosed by serology (IgM/IgG antibody titers) and PCR for direct DNA detection. Culture is avoided because it requires BSL-3 facilities and is hazardous.
explanation.whyCorrect: C. burnetii stains poorly with Gram stain and Giemsa, and cannot be cultured on routine media (it requires cell lines in BSL-3 containment). The best diagnostic approach combines serology (IgM and IgG antibody titers) with PCR for direct detection of C. burnetii DNA. Treatment is often initiated based on clinical suspicion, with labs confirming the diagnosis afterward.

whyWrongByChoice.A: C. burnetii is obligately intracellular and does not grow on standard blood culture media. Culture requires cell lines in BSL-3 facilities and is generally avoided.
whyWrongByChoice.B: C. burnetii stains poorly on Gram stain, and routine sputum culture will not grow this obligate intracellular organism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Urine antigen detection is used for Legionella and S. pneumoniae, not C. burnetii.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While Giemsa stain is sometimes used for intracellular organisms, it produces inconsistent results with C. burnetii. Serology and PCR are the reliable diagnostic methods.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: The 2001 U.S. anthrax attacks involved letters containing highly refined B. anthracis spores. What was the mortality rate among the 11 inhalational anthrax cases despite rapid detection and treatment?

explanation.summary: During the 2001 anthrax attacks, 11 inhalational cases resulted in 5 deaths (~45% mortality) despite early detection and aggressive treatment. This highlights the extreme lethality of inhalation anthrax even with modern medicine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The 2001 U.S. anthrax attacks resulted in 11 inhalational anthrax cases, of which 5 died (approximately 45% mortality), despite knowledge of exposure, early detection, and rapid clinical intervention. This illustrates that even with prompt, aggressive treatment, inhalational anthrax mortality remains high. Untreated inhalational anthrax has >90% mortality.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Five of 11 inhalational anthrax patients died, so the mortality was not 0%.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 10% underestimates the mortality significantly. Nearly half the patients died.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 25% underestimates the actual mortality of approximately 45% (5/11).
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90% is the untreated mortality rate for inhalational anthrax. With treatment in the 2001 cases, mortality was approximately 45%.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Lethal factor (LF), a component of anthrax toxin, causes cell death by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cleaving enzymes in the MAPK pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Forming pores in the cell membrane

explanation.summary: Lethal factor (LF) is a protease that cleaves enzymes in the MAPK pathway, leading to rapid cell death. It is the active (A) component of lethal toxin (PA + LF).
explanation.whyCorrect: Lethal factor (LF) is a zinc-dependent metalloprotease that cleaves enzymes in the MAPK (mitogen-activated protein kinase) pathway, disrupting critical cell signaling and leading to rapid cell death. LF is the active (A) component of lethal toxin (LT = PA + LF). Combined with the cAMP-elevating edema factor, these toxins impair both innate and adaptive immunity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing cellular cAMP is the mechanism of edema factor (EF), not lethal factor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pore formation in the cell membrane is the role of protective antigen (PA), which forms the pore allowing LF and EF to enter cells. LF itself cleaves MAPK enzymes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting ribosomal protein synthesis is the mechanism of various antibiotics (macrolides, aminoglycosides) and some bacterial toxins (diphtheria toxin, Pseudomonas exotoxin A), not anthrax lethal factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LF does not activate the complement cascade. It disrupts host cell signaling through MAPK pathway cleavage.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A patient with Q fever pneumonia has a chest X-ray. Which of the following radiographic findings is most consistent with C. burnetii pneumonia?

explanation.summary: Q fever pneumonia resembles viral or atypical pneumonia on X-ray, with segmental or lobar consolidation (unilateral or bilateral) and interstitial infiltrates with ground-glass opacities. Pleural effusion and cavitation are very rare.
explanation.whyCorrect: C. burnetii pneumonia resembles viral or atypical pneumonia on chest X-ray. Findings include segmental or lobar consolidation (which can be unilateral or bilateral and involve any lobe) and interstitial infiltrates with ground-glass opacities. Pleural effusion or cavitation is very rare. The pneumonia typically lasts 10-90 days.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mediastinal widening is the classic radiographic finding of inhalation anthrax (hemorrhagic mediastinitis), not Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Upper lobe cavitary lesions are characteristic of reactivation tuberculosis and Nocardia, not Q fever pneumonia. Cavitation is very rare in Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy is more associated with sarcoidosis, lymphoma, or primary TB (Ghon complex), not Q fever.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pleural effusions are very rare in Q fever pneumonia. Consolidation and ground-glass opacities are the more typical findings.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: B. anthracis is nonmotile and non-hemolytic. Which of the following Bacillus species is motile and Î²-hemolytic, and causes food poisoning rather than anthrax?

explanation.summary: B. cereus is motile and Î²-hemolytic (unlike B. anthracis which is nonmotile and non-hemolytic). B. cereus causes food poisoning through enterotoxins, not anthrax.
explanation.whyCorrect: B. anthracis is nonmotile and non-hemolytic (Î³-hemolytic); all other Bacillus spp., including B. cereus, are motile and Î²-hemolytic. B. cereus is the second most clinically important Bacillus species, causing food poisoning through heat-stable and heat-labile enterotoxins. The motility and hemolysis pattern help distinguish B. cereus from B. anthracis in the laboratory.

whyWrongByChoice.A: B. subtilis is a common environmental Bacillus but is not the primary species associated with food poisoning alongside B. anthracis as a key comparison organism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: B. megaterium is not a major human pathogen and is not the primary Bacillus species compared with B. anthracis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B. thuringiensis is used as a biological insecticide. It is not a significant human pathogen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: B. licheniformis occasionally causes food poisoning but B. cereus is by far the most clinically relevant comparison to B. anthracis.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: Drinking unpasteurized milk from livestock is a risk factor for which of the following infections?

explanation.summary: Drinking unpasteurized animal products (especially milk) is a risk factor for Q fever because C. burnetii is found in milk, feces, urine, wool, and particularly placentas of livestock.
explanation.whyCorrect: C. burnetii is found in feces, urine, milk, wool, and particularly the placentas of livestock (cows, goats, sheep). Drinking unpasteurized animal products, especially milk, is a recognized risk factor for Q fever. The most common route of infection is inhalation of aerosolized bacteria, but ingestion of contaminated unpasteurized products is also documented.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inhalation anthrax is caused by inhaling B. anthracis spores, not by drinking unpasteurized milk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cutaneous anthrax occurs through direct skin contact with B. anthracis spores, not through consumption of unpasteurized milk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Melioidosis (B. pseudomallei) is acquired from contaminated soil and water in endemic tropical areas, not from unpasteurized milk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Legionnaires' disease is acquired from inhaling contaminated water aerosols, not from drinking unpasteurized milk.

questions.topicName: B. anthracis and C. burnetii
questions.stem: A hospital lab receives a sample suspicious for B. anthracis. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform Gram stain and sensitivity testing in-house

choices.label: C
choices.text: Send to reference lab; contact state health department and CDC

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discard the sample due to biosafety risk

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform acid-fast staining to confirm identity

explanation.summary: Most hospital labs should not attempt to culture B. anthracis. Samples are sent to reference labs for positive identification. Local state health departments and the CDC Emergency Operation Center should be contacted for B. anthracis testing consultations and reporting.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most hospital labs will not (and should not) try to culture B. anthracis. Samples suspected of containing B. anthracis are sent to reference labs for positive identification. Local state departments and the CDC Emergency Operation Center should be contacted for testing consultations and reporting. This is both for safety and for public health surveillance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MacConkey agar is for gram-negative enteric bacteria. B. anthracis is gram-positive and requires blood agar, but more importantly, hospital labs should send samples to reference labs rather than culturing in-house.
whyWrongByChoice.B: In-house Gram stain may be done for preliminary identification, but most hospital labs should not attempt full culture and sensitivity testing for B. anthracis. Reference labs with appropriate containment should handle this.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Discarding the sample would destroy diagnostic evidence and prevent public health investigation. The sample should be preserved and sent to a reference lab.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acid-fast staining is used for mycobacteria, not B. anthracis. B. anthracis is identified by Gram stain (gram-positive spore-forming rod) and culture characteristics (Medusa head colonies).

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 32-year-old missionary returns from a global mission trip with a 3-week cough, night sweats, weight loss, and blood-streaked sputum. Exam shows crackles in the upper lobes and mild lymphadenopathy. A chest radiograph reveals upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which molecular event best explains why his organism survived within alveolar macrophages to produce this presentation?

explanation.summary: M. tuberculosis survives in macrophages by blocking phagosome acidification and lysosome fusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF states M. tuberculosis lives within phagosomes of alveolar macrophages and that survival is due to virulence factors that inhibit both lysosome fusion and acidification, preventing intracellular killing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IgA protease is not described as a mycobacterial virulence factor in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mycobacteria rely on a mycolic acid-rich lipid cell wall, not a polysaccharide capsule.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Exotoxin A is not a listed virulence factor of M. tuberculosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biofilm formation is not described as the survival mechanism for intracellular MTB.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy born in a country with high TB prevalence received the BCG vaccine 3 years ago. He is now being screened before starting school abroad. A PPD skin test at 72 hours shows 12 mm of induration. He is asymptomatic, with a normal chest radiograph. Which screening approach would most accurately determine whether he has latent M. tuberculosis infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeat the PPD skin test in 2 weeks

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nucleic acid amplification test of sputum

explanation.summary: IGRA avoids false positives from BCG and is the preferred screen in BCG-vaccinated patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the text, the IFN-gamma release assay does not produce false positives after BCG vaccination or non-tuberculosis mycobacterium infection, making it the best latent TB screen in a recently BCG-vaccinated patient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Repeat PPD will still be confounded by BCG-induced memory T cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sputum staining and culture are useful only in active disease with productive sputum.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Culture is only useful when the patient has symptoms and produces sputum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NAAT is for active infection with sputum available, not latent screening.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis is about to start a TNF-inhibitor. The physician recommends latent TB screening first. Which immunologic consequence of the planned therapy most directly explains this precaution?

explanation.summary: Immunosuppressants used for autoimmune disease can reactivate latent TB by impairing Th1/IFN-gamma-driven granuloma control.
explanation.whyCorrect: The text notes multiple immunosuppressants used for autoimmune diseases and inflammation increase risk of latent TB reactivation, and that reactivation occurs when Th1 cell number or function is reduced, disrupting granuloma containment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The brick attributes TB control to Th1 cells and macrophages, not B cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mucosal IgA is not described as the key defense against MTB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neutrophil migration is inhibited by cord factor but is not the mechanism of reactivation by immunosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: C3b opsonization is involved in uptake; reactivation is not driven by C3b deficiency per the text.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV (CD4 = 60 cells/mm3) develops persistent fevers, night sweats, cachexia, chronic diarrhea, anemia, and generalized lymphadenopathy. Blood cultures grow an acid-fast organism from an environmental mycobacterium rather than M. tuberculosis. Which cytokines are most responsible for his systemic symptoms?

explanation.summary: Disseminated MAC causes elevated TNF-alpha and IL-6, producing fever, cachexia, night sweats, anemia, and diarrhea.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF explicitly states MAC bacteremia causes elevated serum TNF-alpha and IL-6, resulting in fever, night sweats, cachexia, anemia, diarrhea, and lymph node enlargement.

whyWrongByChoice.B: IL-4/IL-5 drive Th2/eosinophilic responses, not the MAC disseminated syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IL-10/TGF-beta are immunosuppressive and not the drivers of the described picture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IL-2/IL-15 expand lymphocytes but are not cited as MAC disease mediators.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IL-17/IL-22 are not named in the MAC disease mechanism.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A biopsy from a patient's lung lesion shows a granuloma with central caseation surrounded by epithelioid and multinucleate giant cells. Which host defense pathway most directly produced this histologic appearance?

explanation.summary: Cell-mediated immunity via IL-12 and IFN-gamma activates macrophages and drives caseating granuloma formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that infected macrophages secrete IL-12, which activates NK cells and drives Th1 differentiation; IFN-gamma then activates macrophages, which organize into caseating granulomas.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Th2/eosinophils are not the cell-mediated defense described for MTB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IgE/mast cells mediate allergy, not mycobacterial granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Complement assists opsonization via C3b but does not cause caseating granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neutrophils migrate poorly (cord factor) and are not the dominant cell type in caseating TB granulomas.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A patient with HIV and a CD4 count of 40 cells/mm3 develops progressive pulmonary M. tuberculosis. Compared with an immunocompetent host, which specific immunologic deficit most strongly predisposes him to this progression?

explanation.summary: AIDS patients are often IL-12 deficient, which impairs Th1/NK activation needed to control MTB.
explanation.whyCorrect: The clinical correlation states many HIV-infected patients are IL-12 deficient, which is one mechanism by which AIDS patients are susceptible to TB infections.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IgA deficiency is not specified as the mechanism for TB susceptibility in HIV.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Myeloperoxidase is not the deficit described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Terminal complement deficiency predisposes to Neisseria, not MTB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgM deficiency is not the pathway implicated.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A microbiologist cultures sputum on Löwenstein-Jensen medium and notes waxy, long serpentine cord-like colonies. Which virulence factor accounts for both the colony morphology and the impaired neutrophil response at the infection site?

explanation.summary: Cord factor is toxic to mammalian cells, inhibits neutrophil migration, and creates serpentine colonies.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states cord factor is toxic to mammalian cells and inhibits neutrophil migration, and creates long, serpentine colonies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mycolic acid confers antibiotic resistance and immune evasion but is not the serpentine-cord factor described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lipoarabinomannan induces cytokines and resists oxidative stress, not serpentine cord formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catalase-peroxidase is not described in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sulfatide is not mentioned in the brick.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 24-year-old healthcare worker has a PPD skin test read at 72 hours showing 14 mm of induration. She is asymptomatic with a normal chest X-ray. What is the immunologic basis of the induration seen on her arm?

explanation.summary: PPD induration is a delayed type IV hypersensitivity reaction driven by memory T cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF states a positive PPD is a type IV hypersensitivity reaction in which memory T cells acquired from infection are recruited to the injection site, release cytokines, and cause swelling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type I is immediate, IgE-mediated (anaphylaxis), not the 48-72 h induration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type II antibody-mediated cytotoxicity is not the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune complex reactions do not explain PPD induration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Innate neutrophilic response is not the described mechanism.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: An 8-month-old infant with primary M. tuberculosis infection develops progressive cough, failure to thrive, and low-grade fever. His uncontained bacilli are replicating within alveolar macrophages. Which step in intracellular survival most directly permits this continued growth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of macrophage calcium signaling and phagosome acidification

choices.label: B
choices.text: Secretion of hyaluronidase to spread locally

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expression of M protein to block phagocytosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Induction of apoptosis in T cells

explanation.summary: MTB blocks phagosome-lysosome fusion by inhibiting intracellular calcium and phagosome acidification.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick specifies that M. tuberculosis prevents phagosome-lysosome fusion by blocking macrophage intracellular calcium and phagosome/lysosome acidification, permitting intracellular growth within phagosomes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hyaluronidase is not a listed MTB virulence factor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IgG-cleaving protease is not a described MTB mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M protein is a streptococcal antigen, not mycobacterial.
whyWrongByChoice.E: T-cell apoptosis is not described as the mechanism of intracellular growth.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man from a TB-endemic region presents with 4 weeks of cough, fevers, and hemoptysis. A chest radiograph shows upper-lobe cavitary lesions. In the lung pathology specimen, which event most directly accounts for his hemoptysis?

explanation.summary: Reactivation TB causes endothelial damage that leads to hemoptysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The PDF says during reactivation, granulomas break into a bronchus and endothelial damage may also occur, causing hemoptysis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Th2 cytokine capillary leak is not the described mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IgE/mast cell is not the pathology of cavitary TB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Squamous metaplasia is not described as the cause of hemoptysis in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pleural effusion from lymphatic blockage is not the cited cause of hemoptysis.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A patient has a positive PPD and an abnormal chest X-ray. Sputum NAAT is positive for M. tuberculosis. The panel additionally reports rifampicin resistance. Clinicians should be concerned that further testing may also reveal multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB). Which definition best characterizes MDR-TB as described in the brick?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resistance to any two second-line drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resistance to all first- and second-line drugs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resistance only to ethambutol and pyrazinamide

explanation.summary: MDR-TB is defined as resistance to isoniazid and rifampicin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states some MTB strains can be resistant to isoniazid and rifampicin, and such disease is referred to as multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fluoroquinolone resistance plus additional second-line resistance on top of MDR defines XDR-TB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Two second-line drugs alone do not define MDR-TB per the text.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Resistance to all drugs is not the stated definition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ethambutol/pyrazinamide resistance alone is not the described definition.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A strain of M. tuberculosis is identified as resistant to isoniazid, rifampicin, a fluoroquinolone, and at least one additional second-line agent. How is this strain best classified?

explanation.summary: XDR-TB adds fluoroquinolone and at least one other second-line drug resistance to MDR-TB.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines XDR-TB as MDR-TB that is additionally resistant to a fluoroquinolone and at least another second-line drug.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MDR-TB alone means resistance only to isoniazid and rifampicin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pan-susceptible TB is not resistant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary drug-resistant TB is not the defined entity here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Latent resistant TB is not a category in the brick.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A chest X-ray from a patient with active primary TB shows a gray-white parenchymal focus near the pleura in the lower part of the upper lobe, with thickened pleura and caseating hilar lymph nodes. Which term best describes this entire structure?

explanation.summary: Ghon focus plus hilar node involvement and thickened pleura together equal the Ghon complex.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the brick, the Ghon complex consists of the Ghon focus, thickened pleura, and hilar lymphadenopathy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Ghon focus alone is just the parenchymal granuloma, not the entire structure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ranke complex is not described in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Miliary TB refers to disseminated tiny lesions throughout the body.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Simon focus is not described in the brick.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: An autopsy of an immunosuppressed patient reveals innumerable tiny, seed-like granulomas scattered throughout the lungs, liver, and spleen. Which TB phase most commonly underlies this pattern?

explanation.summary: Miliary TB most commonly occurs during reactivation of latent infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states miliary TB most commonly occurs during reactivation of latent infection, though it can also develop during primary infection with severe immunosuppression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Latent TB is asymptomatic with bacilli walled off in granulomas; it does not produce miliary lesions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Primary infection without progression is typically mild or asymptomatic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PPD conversion is an immunologic response, not dissemination.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BCG is a vaccine reaction, not a cause of miliary TB.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A patient with latent TB infection has bacilli walled off within granulomas for years. Which single statement best distinguishes this latent state from active TB in terms of transmission and contact tracing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Latent TB patients are highly infectious by cough

choices.label: D
choices.text: Active and latent TB require identical contact tracing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Latent TB patients are not infectious

explanation.summary: Latent TB is non-infectious; active TB is highly infectious and warrants isolation and contact tracing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes active TB patients are extremely infectious and patients with latent TB are not infectious.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Latent TB patients are not infectious.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Latent TB does not generate productive sputum with AFB.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Negative-pressure isolation is used for active TB, not latent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Contact tracing is emphasized for active disease contacts.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A microbiology resident sees beaded, slightly bent rods on Ziehl-Neelsen stain of a sputum sample. Gram stain of the same sample is unrevealing. Which structural feature best accounts for both findings?

explanation.summary: High mycolic acid content prevents Gram uptake and requires acid-fast staining.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains mycolic acids distinguish mycobacteria from typical bacteria and cannot take up Gram stain, so Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast) staining is required.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Typical bacteria with peptidoglycan take up Gram stain well.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LPS is characteristic of gram-negatives, not mycobacteria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyaluronic acid capsule is a streptococcal feature, not mycobacterial.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Teichoic acids are in gram-positive walls and do not block Gram stain.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A patient with AIDS and chronic diarrhea develops fever, night sweats, weight loss, and generalized lymphadenopathy. Blood cultures grow an acid-fast environmental mycobacterium. Which route of entry best explains the simultaneous pulmonary and GI involvement seen with this organism?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhalation and ingestion from environmental sources

explanation.summary: MAC enters via respiratory and GI cells from environmental sources; not human-to-human.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states patients become infected with MAC by inhaling bacilli from contaminated soil, water, or animals, and the bacilli enter the body through both respiratory and GI cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MAC is not restricted to airborne droplet transmission; M. tuberculosis is.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sexual transmission is not described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transplacental transmission is not mentioned.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vector-borne transmission is not described.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A microbiologist plates sputum on Löwenstein-Jensen media, expecting detection of waxy mycobacterial colonies in 10 days. Compared to E. coli, which metabolic property most directly explains this delay?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 24-hour doubling time and mycolic acid-slowed growth

explanation.summary: Mycobacteria have a 24-hour doubling time because mycolic acids slow growth.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes mycolic acid slows growth resulting in chronic pulmonary infection and that the doubling time in culture is about 24 hours compared with E. coli's 20 minutes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Mycobacteria are obligate aerobes, not anaerobes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Although intracellular, MTB can still be grown on special media; the key reason for slow culture is slow doubling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mycobacteria are non-spore-forming.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mycobacteria are non-motile.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with latent TB and no other risk factors is counseled that his lifetime risk of reactivation is about 5%. Which additional factor from his history would most strongly increase his risk of reactivation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: History of clearing a prior viral URI

explanation.summary: Chronic illnesses like diabetes are known precipitants of TB reactivation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists development of chronic illnesses like diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and lymphoma among the precipitants of TB reactivation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exercise is not described as a reactivation risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Clearing a viral URI is not listed as a risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dietary fiber is not a reactivation risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Influenza vaccination is not a reactivation risk factor.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman had a positive PPD 5 years ago after TB exposure, was treated, and cleared the infection. Today she is rescreened. Her PPD is again positive. Which explanation best accounts for this result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reinfection with new MTB strain is certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Memory T cells persist and react to PPD despite clearance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current active cavitary TB is likely

choices.label: D
choices.text: The PPD is detecting circulating mycolic acid

explanation.summary: PPD can remain positive for years after cleared infection due to persistent memory T cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states the PPD skin test can false-positive if the patient has ever had a Mycobacterium infection that was cleared, since memory T cells persist and react to PPD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A reinfection cannot be concluded from a positive PPD alone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Active cavitary TB requires imaging and symptoms, not just PPD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PPD detects T-cell memory, not circulating mycolic acid.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no described Streptococcus pyogenes cross-reaction.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A pediatric patient is vaccinated with BCG in a high-prevalence TB country. Which statement best summarizes what this vaccine can and cannot do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prevents infection in 80% and reactivation in 90%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Poor at preventing infection but prevents up to 50% of reactivations

choices.label: E
choices.text: Never causes false positives on PPD

explanation.summary: BCG is only about 20% effective at preventing infection but prevents up to 50% of reactivations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the brick, BCG is poor at preventing infection (20% effective) but prevents up to 50% of reactivations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The numbers cited are incorrect per the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BCG does not eradicate latent TB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BCG is not indicated for sterilizing immunity against MAC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BCG can cause false positives on PPD, especially within 10 years of vaccination.

questions.topicName: Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC (Advanced)
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with symptomatic cavitary pulmonary disease has sputum that is AFB-positive. Clinicians want the most rapid combination for confirming active TB while also assessing for drug resistance. Which approach fulfills both goals?
